topics.label: Safety, Assurance & Governance
topics.focus: This topic has two pillars with UNEQUAL weight. Pillar 1 is the PRIMARY focus. PILLAR 1 (PRIMARY — aim for 2 of 3 Key Developments): AI SAFETY RESEARCH & ASSURANCE — alignment research (RLHF, constitutional AI, scalable oversight, weak-to-strong generalization); interpretability and mechanistic interpretability advances; red-teaming methodologies and adversarial robustness; dangerous capability evaluations (cyber, biological, persuasion, autonomous replication) from AISI, METR, Apollo Research, and lab-internal teams; evaluation integrity and benchmark gaming; agentic supervision and control (monitoring, kill switches, CoT faithfulness, sandboxing); jailbreak research and mitigation; responsible AI tooling (guardrails, content filtering, bias detection); AI auditing methodologies and assurance tooling; and safety benchmarks. Search arXiv, lab blogs (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, METR, MIRI, Redwood Research), and safety evaluation bodies (AISI, Apollo Research, CAIS) explicitly. PILLAR 2 (SECONDARY — at most 1 of 3 Key Developments): GOVERNANCE & REGULATION — only include legislation or regulation as a Key Development if it sets a genuinely new precedent (e.g., first law of its kind, first enforcement action, new compliance deadline). Routine legislative movement (committee votes, session scheduling, bill amendments, incremental trilogue progress) belongs in Landscape Trends, not Key Developments. Cover: formal AI governance frameworks (EU AI Act enforcement, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001); multi-jurisdiction regulatory developments; enterprise risk management for LLM deployments. BALANCE RULE: At least 2 of 3 Key Developments MUST come from Pillar 1 (safety research, evaluations, techniques, tooling). If web search returns mostly regulatory news, you have not searched hard enough for safety research — go back and run dedicated searches for lab blogs, AISI, METR, Apollo Research, arXiv safety papers before finalizing.

topics.label: Enterprise GenAI Adoption
topics.focus: how enterprises are deploying GenAI at scale including specific case studies with ROI data, productivity metrics, and failure modes; organizational and workforce change management; build-vs-buy and vendor selection decisions; large-scale regulated industry adoption (financial services, healthcare, legal, government); barriers to production deployment; and analyst forecasts from IDC, Gartner, Forrester, and McKinsey on enterprise AI spend and readiness

topics.label: Agentic Systems
topics.focus: Agent frameworks and agent-specific system design: autonomous agent frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, Mastra, etc.) and their production readiness; multi-agent coordination protocols (MCP, A2A) and ecosystem adoption; tool use, planning, delegation, long-horizon task reliability, and memory architectures (episodic, semantic, procedural) for persistent agent state; agent evaluation benchmarks and failure-mode taxonomies; agentic workflow design patterns; and AgentOps only where the development is specifically about agent behavior, orchestration, or non-deterministic agent evaluation. EXCLUDE FROM THIS TOPIC — generic LLM observability platforms, general-purpose eval tools, serving-framework releases, inference optimization, and broad production infrastructure unless the primary significance is clearly agent-specific (for example: tool-use tracing for agents, multi-agent orchestration, agent memory management, or agent protocol adoption). If the same item would read unchanged in the LLM Production Infrastructure brief, it does not belong here.

topics.label: Models & Market
topics.focus: two distinct but related areas covered together: (1) FRONTIER CAPABILITIES — latest model releases and benchmark results from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta AI, Mistral, DeepSeek, xAI, and others; significant research breakthroughs; independent benchmark evaluations (SWE-bench, GPQA, ARC-AGI, MMMU); context window advances; reasoning and coding capability gaps; and (2) MARKET & PRODUCTS — commercial product launches, API changes, pricing shifts, platform integrations, and competitive positioning; the open-weight vs. closed model dynamic; enterprise platform updates from Microsoft, AWS, Google Cloud, Salesforce; and analyst views on market structure. Cover both lenses in each brief — capability without market context, or market without capability grounding, misses the full picture

topics.label: LLM Production Infrastructure
topics.focus: The full LLM production stack, with priority determined by the reader's role in LLM observability, evaluation, and governance at enterprise scale. Structure coverage accordingly: LEAD WITH (and use for Technical Deep-Dive) — observability and monitoring platforms (Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, etc.); evaluation frameworks for quality, safety, hallucination, and policy compliance; LLMOps tooling and deployment pipelines; production monitoring including drift detection, multi-turn evaluation, and eval-driven alerting; fine-tuning and PEFT workflows; vendor landscape for LLM ops. ALSO COVER (Key Developments bullets, no deep dive needed) — serving framework releases (vLLM, SGLang, TGI) and inference efficiency developments including kernel optimisation, KV cache compression, quantisation — surface what changed and why it matters operationally, but do not deep-dive into implementation details. EXCLUDE FROM THIS TOPIC — AI safety research, alignment, regulatory frameworks, and governance policy (those belong in Safety & Governance); model benchmark results and capability research (those belong in Models & Market)

topics.label: AI Infrastructure & Geopolitics
topics.focus: the physical and geopolitical substrate that determines AI capacity: semiconductor supply chains (TSMC, ASML, HBM memory, CoWoS packaging) and disruption risks; GPU and TPU availability, allocation, and waitlists; energy supply for AI — power consumption trends, data center energy sourcing, nuclear and renewable commitments by hyperscalers; new data center announcements and buildout timelines; sovereign compute initiatives and hyperscaler regional expansion, with particular focus on Australia and Asia-Pacific (when will AWS, Azure, Google, Oracle put meaningful GPU capacity into Australian sovereign territory?); US and allied export controls on advanced chips and their downstream effects on training and inference capacity; geopolitical risks to the AI supply chain (Taiwan, China, trade policy); and macroeconomic factors affecting AI infrastructure investment. Preferred sources: Epoch AI, SemiAnalysis, The Information, IEEE Spectrum, official hyperscaler infrastructure announcements, and energy/policy outlets
